one-to-many-and-many-to-many-results.label: One-to-Many and Many-to-Many Results
one-to-many-and-many-to-many-results.summary: Predict why one parent row may appear several times after joining to related detail rows.

try_one_to_many_and_many_to_many_results.title: Try One Row per Course ID
try_one_to_many_and_many_to_many_results.prompt: Return one row per course with the course id and its department name. Return `courses.id AS course_id, departments.name AS department_name` from `departments` in that column order. Use a INNER JOIN to connect `courses` on `departments.id = courses.department_id`. Sort the final result by `departments.id, courses.id`.
try_one_to_many_and_many_to_many_results.hint: The many side is `courses`, so one output row should represent one course.

help.concept: Read the brief as a result contract: identify the source table or join path, the result columns, the row condition or grouping rule, and any required ordering.
help.hint_1: Build the result one clause at a time and run it after each meaningful change.
help.hint_2: Use a INNER JOIN to connect `courses` on `departments.id = courses.department_id`. Sort the final result by `departments.id, courses.id`.

try_one_to_many_and_many_to_many_results.solutionCode:
SELECT courses.id AS course_id,
       departments.name AS department_name
FROM departments
INNER JOIN courses ON departments.id = courses.department_id
ORDER BY departments.id, courses.id;

try_one_to_many_and_many_to_many_results.title: Try One Row per Enrollment
try_one_to_many_and_many_to_many_results.prompt: Return one row per enrollment with the enrollment id, student grade level, and course title. Return `enrollments.id AS enrollment_id, students.grade_level, courses.title AS course_title` from `enrollments` in that column order. Use a INNER JOIN to connect `students` on `enrollments.student_id = students.id`. Use a INNER JOIN to connect `courses` on `enrollments.course_id = courses.id`. Sort the final result by `enrollments.id`.
try_one_to_many_and_many_to_many_results.hint: Use the bridge row as the grain and include its id in the result.

help.concept: Read the brief as a result contract: identify the source table or join path, the result columns, the row condition or grouping rule, and any required ordering.
help.hint_1: Build the result one clause at a time and run it after each meaningful change.
help.hint_2: Use a INNER JOIN to connect `courses` on `enrollments.course_id = courses.id`. Sort the final result by `enrollments.id`.

try_one_to_many_and_many_to_many_results.starterCode:
SELECT 
FROM students
JOIN enrollments ON 
JOIN courses ON ;
try_one_to_many_and_many_to_many_results.solutionCode:
SELECT enrollments.id AS enrollment_id,
       students.grade_level,
       courses.title AS course_title
FROM enrollments
INNER JOIN students ON enrollments.student_id = students.id
INNER JOIN courses ON enrollments.course_id = courses.id
ORDER BY enrollments.id;

single-choice-1.title: Understanding One-to-Many Relationships
single-choice-1.prompt: In a one-to-many relationship, how many records in the second table can be associated with a single record in the first table?
single-choice-1.hint: Ask what one output row represents.

help.concept: Relationship shape explains why a parent may repeat and what one joined result row represents.
help.hint_1: Identify the detail or bridge row that can repeat for one parent.
help.hint_2: State the result grain in plain language before deciding whether repeated rows are correct.

single-choice-2.title: Identifying Bridge Tables
single-choice-2.prompt: Which table acts as a bridge between students and courses in a many-to-many relationship?
single-choice-2.hint: Ask what one output row represents.

help.concept: Relationship shape explains why a parent may repeat and what one joined result row represents.
help.hint_1: Identify the detail or bridge row that can repeat for one parent.
help.hint_2: State the result grain in plain language before deciding whether repeated rows are correct.

fill-blank-choice-1.prompt: Complete the SQL statement to join `students` and `enrollments` on the student ID.
fill-blank-choice-1.hint: Use the correct SQL syntax for joining tables.

help.concept: The `ON` clause specifies the condition for joining tables.
help.hint_1: The `ON` clause should match the primary key of one table to the foreign key of another.
help.hint_2: Ensure the column names are correctly qualified with table names.

fill-blank-choice-2.title: Name the Result Grain
fill-blank-choice-2.prompt: After joining departments to courses without grouping, what does one result row represent?
fill-blank-choice-2.hint: Read the selected detail table and identify which row can repeat for one department.

help.concept: Result grain describes what one output row represents; it is not determined by a relationship label alone.
help.hint_1: A department can own several course rows.
help.hint_2: The ungrouped joined result has one row for each matching course.

fill-blank-choice-2.template: One result row represents [blank1] together with its department.

multi-choice-1.title: Characteristics of Many-to-Many Relationships
multi-choice-1.prompt: Select all characteristics of a many-to-many relationship.
multi-choice-1.hint: Consider how many-to-many relationships are structured.

help.concept: Many-to-many relationships involve a bridge table to connect two tables.
help.hint_1: Think about the role of the bridge table.
help.hint_2: Consider how each table can relate to multiple records in the other.

options.b: Each record in one table can relate to multiple records in the other
options.c: Directly connects two tables without intermediaries

drag-reorder-1.title: Order of SQL Join Operations
drag-reorder-1.prompt: Arrange the steps to perform a join between `students` and `courses` using `enrollments`.
drag-reorder-1.hint: Think about the sequence of joining tables in a many-to-many relationship.

help.concept: Joining tables in a many-to-many relationship involves using a bridge table.
help.hint_1: Start by joining the first table to the bridge table.
help.hint_2: Then join the bridge table to the second table.

tokens.t3: Select columns from students and courses

sketch0.title: One-to-many joins repeat the row from the one side
sketch0.bodyMarkdown:
A **one-to-many relationship** means that one row in a parent table may be connected to several rows in another table, while each of those related rows points back to one parent. Departments and courses have this shape: one department can offer many courses, but each course belongs to one department.

```sql
SELECT departments.name AS department_name,
       courses.title AS course_title
FROM departments
INNER JOIN courses
  ON departments.id = courses.department_id;
```

The query produces one row for each matched course. If Humanities offers two courses, the department name appears twice—once beside each course title. SQL has not duplicated a department by mistake. It has repeated the department value because the **result grain is one course with its department**.

This distinction matters whenever a joined result contains repeated labels. Before reaching for `DISTINCT`, ask what one row is supposed to represent. If each row represents a different course, the repeated department name is correct and useful.

The ERD's `1` and `many` markers help predict this pattern before the query runs. The side marked `many` usually determines how many detailed rows can appear when no grouping changes the grain.

sketch1.title: The bridge row becomes the grain of a many-to-many result
sketch1.bodyMarkdown:
A **many-to-many relationship** exists when rows on both sides may have several partners. Students can take many courses, and courses can include many students. The database represents that idea through `enrollments`, where each row stores one particular student-course link.

```sql
SELECT students.name AS student_name,
       courses.title AS course_title
FROM students
INNER JOIN enrollments
  ON students.id = enrollments.student_id
INNER JOIN courses
  ON enrollments.course_id = courses.id;
```

The path begins at a student, crosses one enrollment row, and arrives at one course. Because the bridge records the relationship, one output row represents **one enrollment**, even though the selected columns come from `students` and `courses`.

Repetition can now occur on both sides. Mia may appear beside several course titles, and Biology may appear beside several student names. Removing those repeated names would erase real relationships. The important question is whether the combination of values represents a distinct bridge row.

This idea prepares you for counts later in the course. Counting output rows measures enrollments. Counting distinct students or distinct courses measures a different entity and may require `COUNT(DISTINCT ...)`.
